retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Pneumothorax -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Spontaneous pneumothorax is the presence of air within the pleural space that occurs without trauma or other iatrogenic causes. This condition is broadly classified into 2 types: primary spontaneous pneumothorax (PSP), which occurs in individuals without known underlying lung disease, and secondary spontaneous pneumothorax (SSP), which develops in patients with existing pulmonary pathology such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, cystic fibrosis, or interstitial lung disease. Patients typically present with the acute onset of pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, and tachycardia. In severe cases, spontaneous pneumothorax can progress to a life-threatening tension pneumothorax, requiring immediate intervention. Diagnosis is primarily clinical, supported by imaging, most often chest radiography or point-of-care ultrasound.

retrieved_docs.title: Air Leak -- Introduction -- Pneumothoraxes Classifications
retrieved_docs.content: Spontaneous pneumothorax These occur without any precipitating event and is divided into two following groups: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax occurs on healthy, non-diseased lungs. Secondary spontaneous pneumothorax develops from lungs that are diseased with changes in the parenchymal structures. Traumatic pneumothorax These pneumothoraxes occur from traumatic injury to the lung and pleura space. The traumatic injury may be direct or indirect. Iatrogenic In critically ill patients, iatrogenic pneumothorax causing an air leak can occur from the following conditions:

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pneumothorax Evaluation and Treatment -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Spontaneous pneumothorax: Occurs without external trauma and is divided into 2 categories. Primary spontaneous pneumothorax: Develops in individuals without underlying lung disease Secondary spontaneous pneumothorax: Occurs in individuals with significant underlying parenchymal lung disease, such as COPD or cystic fibrosis), often triggered by events such as bleb rupture. [14] Pneumothorax can also be classified into 4 categories based on its physiological impact: Simple pneumothorax No communication with the outside atmosphere No mediastinal or diaphragmatic shift Example: A pleural laceration from a fractured rib [15] Communicating pneumothorax Occurs when a defect in the chest wall creates an open connection between the pleura space and the external environment, such as a gunshot wound Results in paradoxical lung collapse and severe ventilatory impairment [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Pneumothorax -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A pneumothorax is a collection of air outside the lung but within the pleural cavity. It occurs when air accumulates between the parietal and visceral pleurae inside the chest. The air accumulation can apply pressure on the lung and make it collapse (see Image. Upper Lobe Pneumothorax). The degree of collapse determines the clinical presentation of pneumothorax (see Image. Right Tension Pneumothorax Radiograph). Air can enter the pleural space by two mechanisms, either by trauma causing communication through the chest wall or from the lung by rupture of the visceral pleura. There are two types of pneumothorax: traumatic and atraumatic. The two subtypes of atraumatic pneumothorax are primary and secondary. A primary spontaneous pneumothorax (PSP) occurs automatically without a known eliciting event, while a secondary spontaneous pneumothorax (SSP) occurs after an underlying pulmonary disease. A traumatic pneumothorax can be the result of blunt or penetrating trauma. Pneumothoraces can be even further classified as simple, tension, or open. A simple pneumothorax does not shift the mediastinal structures, as does a tension pneumothorax (see Image . Left-Sided Tension Pneumothorax Radiograph). Open pneumothorax is an open wound in the chest wall through which air moves in and out. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Chest Pain -- Treatment / Management -- Pneumothorax (PTX)
retrieved_docs.content: Pneumothorax should be decompressed with a chest tube. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Pneumothorax -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of tension pneumothorax is challenging to determine as one-third of cases in trauma centers have decompressive needle thoracostomies before reaching the hospital, and not all of these had tension pneumothorax. Pneumomediastinum has an incidence of 1 case per 10,000 admissions in the hospital.

retrieved_docs.title: Pneumothorax -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: If the patient is discharged from the hospital after a resolved pneumothorax, recommendations should be made for no flying or scuba diving for a minimum of two weeks. Patients with a known history of spontaneous pneumothorax should not be medically cleared for occupations involving flying or scuba diving.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pneumothorax Evaluation and Treatment -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Pneumothorax is categorized into 3 broad types based on its etiology and pathophysiology—spontaneous, traumatic, and tension. Spontaneous pneumothorax occurs without an apparent external cause and is further divided into primary and secondary types. Primary spontaneous pneumothorax develops in individuals without underlying lung disease, most commonly in young, tall, thin males with subclinical pleural blebs. In contrast, secondary spontaneous pneumothorax occurs in patients with preexisting lung conditions such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), cystic fibrosis, or pulmonary fibrosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Tension Pneumothorax -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Atraumatic pneumothorax may be primary with an unknown cause or secondary to underlying lung disease. Recognition of both traumatic and atraumatic causes is essential, as tension pneumothorax can arise from any of these conditions or interventions, necessitating vigilance across clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Pneumothorax -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The disposition of patients with spontaneous pneumothorax is multifactorial. For patients with a first-time episode of PSP that is small and asymptomatic, conservative measures can be a reasonable option. For patients with a first-time episode of PSP that is large and/or symptomatic, aspiration can be attempted as discussed in the treatment/management section; however, failure should prompt admission. The current recommendation is that all patients with SSP, regardless of the stability of the patient and the size of the pneumothorax, should be admitted. Even with proper management, the recurrence rate for spontaneous pneumothorax is relatively high. Some study results estimate a recurrence rate of over 50% with the highest risk within the first 30 days. However, the recurrence rate can be less than 5% after VATS with resection of blebs/bullae and pleurodesis. [7] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Pneumothorax -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: PSP is usually benign and mostly resolves independently without any significant intervention. Recurrence can occur for up to three years period. The recurrence rate in the following five years is 30% for PSP and 43% for SSP. The risk of recurrence increases with each subsequent pneumothorax; it is 30% with the first, 40% after a send, and more than 50% after the third recurrence. PSP is not considered a significant health threat, but deaths have been reported. SSPs are more lethal depending on underlying lung disease and the size of the pneumothorax. Patients with COPD and HIV have high mortality after pneumothorax. The mortality of SSP is 10%. Mortality of tension pneumothorax is high if appropriate measures are not taken.

retrieved_docs.title: Tension Pneumothorax -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The following are the key points in managing tension pneumothorax: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis. The condition may arise from traumatic and atraumatic causes and prehospital and in-hospital settings. If a patient is hemodynamically unstable and a tension pneumothorax is likely, needle decompression must be performed immediately, followed by CTT. If the patient is stable, diagnostic imaging such as a chest x-ray may be obtained before treatment. Patients with pulmonary conditions predisposing them to high peak inspiratory pressures are at greater risk of tension pneumothorax. CTT suffices for managing most pneumothorax cases. Further surgical treatments, including VATS with pleurodesis, may be necessary in others. Quick diagnosis and management can prevent significant morbidity and mortality from this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Iatrogenic Pneumothorax -- Treatment / Management -- Management Algorithm for Iatrogenic Pneumothorax
retrieved_docs.content: Definitive treatment options: Needle aspiration Chest tube drainage VATS Open surgical intervention

retrieved_docs.title: Pneumothorax -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory failure or arrest Cardiac arrest Pyopneumothorax Empyema Rexpansion pulmonary edema Pneumopericardium Pneumoperitoneum Pneumohemothorax Bronchopulmonary fistula Damage to the neurovascular bundle during tube thoracostomy Pain and skin infection at the site of tube thoracostomy

retrieved_docs.title: Iatrogenic Pneumothorax -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Tension pneumothorax is the most notable complication of any pneumothorax previously described. This disorder is life-threatening and requires immediate intervention. [22] Another well-described complication of pneumothorax is having a persistent air leak and failure of lung re-expansion, which usually requires further surgical intervention. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Tension Pneumothorax -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Tension pneumothorax is life-threatening. In patients who survive this condition, complications may arise from the lung injury itself or CTT, which include the following: Pneumopericardium Pneumoperitoneum Hemothorax Bronchopulmonary fistula Damage to the neurovascular bundle during CTT Pain and skin infection at the CTT site Empyema Pyopneumothorax Timely diagnosis and management help improve outcomes in patients with tension pneumothorax.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1001
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is classified as a subdivision of the thorax. This subdivision of the thorax is directly linked to the thoracic conditions pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. The three pathologies—pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse—are also associated with the term “دبيلة”. Recognizing that closed drainage of chest is employed to treat pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse underscores its importance in thoracic management and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 862
retrieved_docs.content: دبيلة is associated with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, indicating that individuals with دبيلة may develop these respiratory complications. Recognizing this relationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and appropriate management of patients presenting with دبيلة.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1623
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is classified as a subdivision of thorax, representing a specific thoracic intervention used to treat conditions such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. By placing a closed drainage system within the thoracic cavity, clinicians can evacuate air, fluid, or blood, thereby re‑expanding the lung and relieving the pathological processes associated with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. This procedure exemplifies how targeted thoracic techniques are applied to manage distinct intrathoracic disorders, highlighting the interrelationship between procedural categories (subdivision of thorax) and the diseases they address.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: The term “hrudník,” referring to the chest, denotes the anatomical location where pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse can develop. These three thoracic conditions are also linked with “دبيلة,” indicating an association between the clinical manifestations of pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse and the entity دبيلة. Understanding that the chest (hrudník) is the site of these pathologies and recognizing their connection to دبيلة is important for accurate diagnosis and effective management of patients presenting with pleural and pulmonary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 974
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is an indirect procedure performed at the anatomical region referred to as hrudník (the chest). The hrudník serves as the location of several thoracic pathologies, including pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. This procedure is also linked to the Subdivision of thorax, a categorical segment of the thoracic cavity that itself has a relationship with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. Together, these connections underscore that closed chest drainage is specifically directed toward the chest area and the thoracic subdivision that are commonly affected by pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, highlighting the procedural relevance to these conditions in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2137
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest has a relationship that it is a subdivision of thorax, indicating that this closed‑system chest tube technique is classified within thoracic procedures. As a subdivision of thorax, closed drainage of chest is employed to evacuate air, fluid, or blood from the pleural space in conditions such as pneumothorax, hemothorax, or pleural effusion, and its categorization guides clinicians in selecting appropriate thoracic interventions and patient‑care strategies.

These procedures target pleural pathologies (abscess, hemothorax, empyema) and mediastinal complications, providing coordinated diagnostic and therapeutic pathways for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 280
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is an indirect procedure performed at the hrudník (the chest). The hrudník serves as the anatomical location for pleurisy; pneumothorax; pulmonary collapse. Closed drainage of chest is also linked to the Subdivision of thorax, which in turn has a relationship with pleurisy; pneumothorax; pulmonary collapse. Moreover, pleurisy; pneumothorax; pulmonary collapse are associated with دبيلة. Recognizing the connection between the procedural site (hrudník), the thoracic subdivision, and the associated term دبيلة supports appropriate use of closed chest drainage in the treatment of pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
retrieved_docs.content: The subdivision of thorax, identified in Arabic as دبيلة, corresponds to the pleural membrane and is directly linked to pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. These three conditions all involve the pleura: pleurisy denotes inflammation of the pleural layers, pneumothorax refers to the presence of air within the pleural space that disrupts normal lung expansion, and pulmonary collapse (atelectasis) often results from pleural pathology that prevents the lung from remaining inflated. Recognizing that the subdivision of thorax (دبيلة) is the anatomical structure underlying these disorders is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic intervention, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: Closed chest drainage is classified within the thoracic subdivision of procedures that involve incision of the pleura, thoracentesis, and chest drainage. This subdivision is directly linked to pleural‑cavity pathologies such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, which are considered manifestations of a broader side‑related disorder (اضطراب جنبي) and are also associated with the term دبيلة. Interventional procedures targeting the pleural space include creation of a pleuroperitoneal shunt, side‑cavity aspiration (رشف التجويف الجنبي), insertion of an underwater‑seal chest drain, and tube thoracostomy with a water seal for indications such as abscess, hemothorax, and empyema. Tube thoracostomy and chest tube insertion (تركيب أنبوب الصدر) are narrower procedures that fall under the overarching category of incision of pleura; thoracentesis; chest drainage, with the latter three actions (incision of pleura, thoracentesis, chest drainage) being directly related to chest‑tube placement. Pleural neoplasms of uncertain or unknown behavior are also linked to incision of the chest wall and pleura within this hierarchy. Understanding the network of thoracic anatomical subdivisions, pleural diseases, and their associated diagnostic and therapeutic interventions is essential for effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 140
retrieved_docs.content: of chest: mass of mid mediastinum,” “mass of posterior mediastinum,” “mass of superior mediastinum,” “mediastinal mass,” “mediastinal mass containing calcium,” and “mediastinal effusion.” All mass‑related entities are classified as “كتلة منصفية” (mediastinal mass), whereas the effusion is classified as “اضطراب منصفي” (mediastinal disorder). Finally, the clinical syndrome “دبيلة” is associated with the triad of pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, linking these respiratory complications to a specific disease entity. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how precise anatomical localization—mediastinum, chest, and thoracic subdivisions—drives the classification, imaging, and intervention of lower‑respiratory and mediastinal pathologies, supporting accurate diagnosis and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 109

Closed chest drainage, thoracentesis, and related interventions are linked to the thorax subdivision and to the mediastinum through imaging findings. Overall, the knowledge base integrates anatomical locations (mediastinum, pleural cavity), disease categories (other lower respiratory disease, mediastinal disorder, pleural disorder), imaging findings (mediastinal masses, effusion, fistulas), and therapeutic procedures (chest tube placement, pleuroperitoneal shunt, underwater‑seal drain) to support accurate diagnosis and effective patient care.
